asset_type: TaraHome technical blog cover for changing a Wi-Fi router without breaking Matter or Thread devices
prompt:
Use case: photorealistic-natural
Asset type: unique TaraHome technical blog cover, landscape editorial photograph
Primary request: show a homeowner safely replacing the household Wi-Fi router while an existing local Matter and Thread smart home remains in place
Scene/backdrop: a warm lived-in residential console or small utility nook with natural oak; two clearly separate generic unbranded consumer Wi-Fi routers sit side by side, the older router slightly dusty and unplugged and the newer router clean but ordinary; a homeowner's hands are deliberately moving one real Ethernet cable from the old router toward the new router; nearby, a compact unbranded local home-automation computer remains powered, with a modest generic smart plug and contact sensor resting in the scene to represent existing devices
Style/medium: photorealistic editorial interior photography with believable everyday textures and subtle imperfections, not a product catalog render and not a diagram
Composition/framing: 3:2 landscape, medium-wide slightly overhead view; keep both routers, the cable handoff, local computer, and small smart-home accessories clear around the center so the scene survives centered 16:10 article and 16:9 card crops; make the old-versus-new swap understandable from physical placement alone
Lighting/mood: soft late-morning window light, calm prepared practical mood
Color palette: warm cream and natural oak with matte white, charcoal, and restrained dark-green accents in the Ethernet cable and a small fabric tray
Materials/textures: realistic oak grain, woven fabric, matte plastic, natural hands, ordinary cable wear, soft true-to-life shadows
Constraints: generic physically plausible devices only; no recognizable brand, product silhouette, logo, app screen, interface, legible text, serial number, setup code, watermark, or title; old router and new router must be visibly distinct physical units without invented branding
Avoid: embedded headline or title poster, fake chart or graph filler, decorative routing/network/path line overlays, glowing connection lines, translucent panels, inset frames, floating UI cards, progress bars, split-screen composition, generic AI-looking diagrams, futuristic control room, glossy CGI, excessive device lineup, invented branded hardware, deformed hands, duplicate or fused routers, impossible cable connections
